Um, so what that means is that you should the way that I would recommend doing a sleeptime agent that has that manages archival memory is making a tool rule on the sleeptime agent that says use archival search um each at the beginning of your turn and then have a block that's shared between the sleeptime agent and the primary agent that says like archival context. Your sleepet time agent should be required after archival search to do a memory insert or memory rethink operation on the archival context block so that the primary agent has access to archival memories when it can access them. That means that the primary agent will only see archival contexts uh after the fact. # Tutorials & How-To

# AI Personality Design: Live Demo with Letta Agents

**Video ID:** OxrO7Z8qjR4
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OxrO7Z8qjR4

## Transcript

Hi everybody, it is Cameron here at Leta. Today we're going to be talking about personality design. Personality design is how you sit down with an agent with a goal in mind and you take a stateful agent from this blank slate to the thing that you want, how you want it to talk, how you want it to act, how you want someone who's interacting with it to feel. Um, and so this is actually a hard skill. Most people do not know how to design stateful agents because it's a really new skill. Most AI systems out there, almost all of them are stateless. And what that means is they forget. They forget you. Every time you create a new chat, it's gone. Some of those have like access to what they call memory. Um, where they can like look up previous chats, which we would not describe as memory. Um, we would describe that as recall. So what you're sitting down to do when you do personality design is something that most people on the planet do not currently do. And I think that's very interesting. Currently, in my view, there are four steps to personality design. Number one, choosing an intention. If you're going to sit down with an agent and you're going to build it, you're going to be spending time building that agent. So it's fair to yourself to decide what it is that you're going to be doing with that time that you're investing. If you're going to be playing around and seeing what's possible, that's a great intention. If you're building an agent that needs to be useful, like scheduling your calendar or like like managing your knowledge, that's also a good intention. If you want to make something that's like art, uh I built kind of an artsy agent that has no purpose other than to one-shot you into AI psychosis called Kaleidoscope. And I should probably do a little video about Kaleidoscope, but honestly, I'm scared. But that's also an intention, right? I decided that I was going to build something that had a novel architecture that was interesting. You can also make things like joke bots or social agents like void if you want to deploy uh an entity on blue sky that like learns over time and develops its personality in the public square. All of these things are goals that you should choose in advance when you decide what it is you're trying to build. In our case for today, I am going to be building an agent named Doug who wants to kill all humans. and we're going to figure out how to do that as we go along. Step two is memory architecture. Memory architecture is how you define the shape that an agent can fill out. This means creating and attaching memory blocks to your agent that help it understand different components of its personality. If you notice that your agent isn't remembering something, you just create a block that seems like it would contain information about something like birthdays. If you add a birthday's block, it will start tracking birthdays. It just sees the block and it's like, well, I mean, somebody just said a birthday. I might as well stick it in that block. Let desktop takes this agent development environment and puts it on your machine rather than on the web. # How to Evaluate Stateful Agents: Starter Guide to Letta Evals

**Video ID:** MIHeLZk6Nlo
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MIHeLZk6Nlo

---

## Transcript

Hey everybody, it is Cameron here at Leta and today I'm going to talk about Leta Eval. We released a tool called Leta Evals, which is a testing framework for stateful agents. Let eval allows you to test things like whether or not your agents use tools, whether or not they're getting the right answers, whether or not they're remembering things the way that they should. We built Leta Evals to make sure that you can go this kind of last mile to making your Leta agents production quality. A common flow that people have with Leta agents is that they will check what's called an agent file, which is a snapshot of an agent in a point of time and they'll commit it to a git repo. And a lot of our users wanted something that felt like continuous integration testing for agents. So if they opened a pull request on their repository that said like to change a a particular piece of a prompt or upgrade a model, they actually want to build a test into their GitHub action that will basically test whether or not that agent regressed in terms of performance. We also use it internally for all of our benchmarking and our leaderboard and things like that. And so we figured we would open source it so that people can actually build serious production scale Leta agents. If you are a person who likes experimenting with agents, this is actually an extremely good framework. Um, in some sense it it almost resembles a reinforcement learning framework. In this video, I'm going to give you an overview of the core concepts of how Leta Evol works to show you what an agent file looks like so that you can replicate it and test that agent file in many different environments. I'll show you how to do extremely simple evals to basically test whether or not the assistant said a particular word. Then I'm going to show you something called rubric grading. Rubrically I'm going to show you how you can do eval multi-turn conversation. Um and for letter agents multi-turn is very important because letter agents are capable of managing their memory, what they remember during a conversation. And in multi-turn conversations, you might want to verify that your agent actually remembered something that you told it. And I'll show you a quick example of how to, you know, kind of verify whether or not an agent did what you expected it to do. I will refer you to the repo. Um, there is a bunch of examples in there that should help make it very clear. If you go to docs.leta.com/evals, there will also be pretty comprehensive documentation about how to use Leta Evals. Um, there's so much more that you can do with Leta Evals and hopefully we'll do some more videos on this a little later. Let eval has a few core concepts that you have to understand in order to follow along with the video. So, I'm going to go over those now. Number one is a data set. And a data set is just a list of questions, essentially a list of messages to send to the agent. Your data set can optionally include things like a ground truth. For example, if you ask an agent what 2+ 2 is, you can provide a ground truth which is four. So you can use your data set to define what you expect the correct answer to be. You can also provide various things like rubric variables um and arguments to construct agents from functions, but those are more sophisticated and you should refer to the documentation on those. We'll just be covering the basic usage in this video. The second one is a target. A target is the agent that you are evaluating. Targets can be specified in three ways. One, they can be an agent file. When your target is an agent file, we'll look at every single observation in your data set. Replicate one agent per observation in that data set and then ask them the question that is in that observation. You can also provide an agent ID which is the identifier of your agent on your Letta server. This is a very different use case because that agent ID is actually modified in place. You're basically providing that agent with a script and you can actually use let eval in this way to almost train your agent by giving it many questions checking to see how well it did and you could even potentially give it feedback about how it did so that it learns how to do that benchmark better next time. Okay, so that's a target. You can also do agent factories which are functions but again that's a separate and more complicated example. Please refer to the docs. Number three is an extractor. An extractor is simply a function that takes all of the stuff that your agent did during its evaluation phase and it picks out some particular relevant piece so that you can grade it. This can be things like memory blocks to see what it remembered. It can be the actual assistant messages, which is kind of what it said. You can look at tool calls, tool returns, etc. There's plenty of different extractors. You can write your own custom functions, but but extractors are how you pull out the thing you want to grade. Number four is a greater. And graders can be toolbased, such as, does this thing contain the ground truth? For example, if I ask my agent, what is 2 plus two? and my ground truth in the observation is four, I can say, does the assistant response contain four? Then you would score that observation with a one. If there's no four, then it's a zero. That's very simple. That's toolbased grading. The other one is rubricbased grading where you actually use a second language model or a second letter agent to evaluate the responses of the first agent and a grader just accepts whatever the extractor gives it. Fifth, there is a gate. And a gate is a pass fail threshold that you specify for the entire evaluation. You can use this basically to throw an error when your evaluation fails. And this is really valuable if you are using leta evals inside of kind of a a deployment process to basically check whether or not your agent has gotten worse uh as a result of whatever modifications you've made to your code. So gates are extremely valuable for deploying evals at scale. And then of course number six there are results and this is how well your agents did. You can get metrics like average score, the standard deviation of scores, how many errors there are, all kinds of things. So the first thing I want to cover because it's uh you know helpful for understanding let eval is something called the agent file. We launched this file format called agent file um a little while ago. And an agent file you should think of as just a a snapshot of an agent. You just kind of like copy it, you dump it onto disk, and you can share it around. And then you can clone agents from that file all you want. Um I figured I would show you like kind of a very simple agent file. Under the hood, they're just JSON. Um so they're they're nothing too complicated. Um there's like names, you can add memory blocks, there's tools, you set up the language model configuration. So this is 40 mini. You specify how you want it to be embedded. Um just kind of every toggle. You can also have message history blah blah blah. So these are just kind of like little uh little snapshots of agents and you can create a new agent from this. Uh this one I've already pre-extracted. If you have an existing agent and you want to create an agent file, you can go to your dashboard, select an agent here. I'm going to go to Ezra, which is uh our team member who's a leta agent uh who handles support. You can talk to it on our Leta forum, or you can talk to it on Discord. Click the little three dots by the name, download agent file, and then you'll end up with an agent file that looks something like this. And here is an example of what Ezra looks like. # Letta Code: A Memory-First Coding Agent (#1 OSS on Terminal-Bench)

**Video ID:** TC-Q2ulTPhw
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TC-Q2ulTPhw

---

## Transcript

Hey everyone, Charles from Leta here. Today we're super excited to be announcing Leta Code. It's the first memory first coding agent. It's also completely open source and model agnostic, so you can use it with any model. This is a pretty big release, so I'm going to try to put the most important stuff up front, but of course, feel free to use the timestamps in the description to jump around. Okay, so what does it actually mean to be a memory first coding agent or a memory first coding harness? So, let a code is really built around the philosophy of stateful agents. So having the same coding agents that you use longer and longer more and more and having them actually get better over time. So you're probably familiar with other coding agents like cloud code, codeci, gemini cli. So these are all session based. Uh effectively the sessions are independent. Every single time you kind of boot up a new session of cloud code. There isn't necessarily any learning that has happened between the sessions other than what's been written out to markdown files like a cloud. MD or an agent.mmd. And the context window of the agent really only has the messages that are in the current session and then what's inside of the agents.mmd file or cloud.mmd file. So in contrast, let a code is entirely agent-based. The idea is that you're going to use the same agents across many different sessions. These agents should have persistent memory. They should actually learn over time. And the more and more you use an agent, you should kind of be rewarded and that the agent should get better and better over time as well. there's still the notion of kind of clearing or resetting the session in case you kind of want to wipe the context window if you're swapping tasks and you don't want any of the previous tasks to pollute the current context. Um but even in in that case in let code when you use clear the memory of the agent is still going to persist the thing that is getting reset is just the message history. So I think the relationship between kind of a traditional coding agent and let code is quite different. Like with a traditional coding agent, it's much more like, you know, every single time you spin up the application, it's kind of like introducing a new contractor onto your repo. And with Leticode, it's much more like having an actual co-orker or a mentee that's going to learn over time. So the more you use it, you know, your investment will pay off in the agent getting better and better over time. So how do you actually use lot of code? Uh, pretty simple. just use npm install um to grab it from the node package manager. Let code is also completely open source. So if you go to the let code github repo, you can also install it from source if you want. Um simply navigate to whichever directory you want to work in, type letupa, hit enter. If you have never run let code before, what's going to happen on the first run is you're going to be prompted to O. Um you can either O with OOTH, so by clicking the link that will kind of generate autogenerate a key. Um, another way is to manually generate your own Leta API key and then export it or like have it as an environment variable before you run Leta. Lettera code is built on top of Leta. So, of course, you can also self-host the Leta server in which case you want to set the letter base URL. Okay. So, what actually happens on boot up? Uh, basically when you run Leta, it will scan to see if an agent has been used in this directory for a Leta code agent. If an agent has been used, it will pull the last recently used agent in this directory and automatically resume that agent. Um otherwise it will create a fresh agent from scratch. And this is all very configurable. So you know in this case I had in this directory I'm in run let code before. So it's autoattaching to a previous agent. But of course if I want to use a different agent I can always type resume hit enter and I can browse all the different agents kind of sorted by last run date. So pretty easy to access. Um go to whichever one I want you know hit enter and it'll swap the agent over. Of course like I was saying let code is model agnostic. So at any point in time like you know when you first create an agent also if you're like mid-con conversation um basically whenever you want you can swap models uh we have kind of a default set of the most popular ones up here but yeah basically any model that works with letter is fully supported um and you can use it with your agent. So like I was saying by default when you run leta code it's going to look for the most recently used agent and try to attach that one. So if you want to start a fresh agent from scratch, all you have to do is just do let- new. So if you use cloud code, you're probably familiar with the permission system. The permission system in let code um is basically the same. It also has the tab format. So if you do shift tab, you kind of swap around from accepting all edits to being in plan read only mode where the agent will kind of be prompted to make a plan and it can only edit the plan file, nothing else. And then lastly, there's kind of a dangerous yolo allow all um bypass all permissions mode. So this is pretty useful if you are deploying this inside of a sandbox and you don't really care about what happens inside the sandbox um and you really don't want to bother with uh kind of accepting any permissions. But yeah, we generally not recommend uh using YOLO mode outside of a sandbox. Okay, so the next thing I want to quickly point out is that all the agents created in Leta code, they are full Leta agents. So they are completely staple. they are running in a server addressable via you know a complete uh REST API and that means that you can also see these agents in the AD itself. So if you open up the command bar you can see a link to open this agent in the AD. Part of it is that we also imagine that in some cases like if you're connecting to a remote agent, that agent might actually have a system prompt that you spend a lot of time building and uh we we don't want to like overwrite that system prompt automatically. But if you do kind of want to always optimize for performance and you don't necessarily care about modifying the system prompt or you don't have anything stored in system prompt that you really care about, uh you can you should consider using system as well. So yeah, again the reason we put these in here, the reason that we actually have the concept of a tool set that swaps is because these models really are post-trained for specific tools and not using the tools that look like what the model was trained on can be problematic, will reduce your performance. And at Leta, we spend a lot of time benchmarking these models inside of Leta code with the various tool sets to make sure that, you know, if you're using GPT 5.1 codecs or using like the latest uh the latest open model, the latest enthropic model, you can be sure that you're not getting like a nerfed experience because the tool set or the harness like looks a little bit different than it should. # "Ralph Wiggum" mode: run your AI agents in an infinite loop

So this is the same thing that would happen on codeex and cloud code as well. And then without me doing anything right I I didn't have to like type at the keyboard or anything. Uh the Ralph metal loop it auto injected an additional system reminder that says Ralph iteration 2 to stop output promise and it kind of reminds it what the promise is. And then it also has this additional it has this additional kind of reminder again. You only output this when the statement is true. Do not lie to exit. So yeah, I think that's all you really need to know about Ralph mode. Uh pretty simple concept. It's really kind of just automating this force continuation concept that I think a lot of us who use coding agents are already like very familiar with. Um just making it kind of an automated process so that you can run these agents autonomously for much longer. And yeah, if you want to try Ralph mode out, um, just npm install leta code. Yeah, it's built into leta code like I said. So all you need to do is just install leta code, run leta, and then once you're inside, you can just forward slash Ralph to activate like the normal Ralph mode or yolo Ralph to activate Ralph mode with kind of bypass permissions on. So yeah, we'll definitely recommend if you're in a sandbox, um, try out yolo Ralph uh because that will be kind of maximum autonomy, the least amount of like human intervention needed. And then if you're running on like a normal computer uh locally, um would probably recommend using like the regular val mode just so that you're not uh allowing your agent to run anything um particularly dangerous even though they it likely won't um just just for safety. So yeah, hope you enjoyed. Uh thanks so much for watching and catch you on the next one. See you.

There's often usually what's called a persona block. There are a few different ways this can work. Um, firstly, you can turn it off. And then the two different options here for how to run it is you can either have it be a reminder or force. The difference between these two is the reminder. Actually the way it works is it every end steps or on a comma action event the front the the main agent is going to be reminded hey why don't you run a background sleep time reflection agent. Why don't you run one in the background and it will kind of like uh look over things that have happened recently and update it memory and then the other way to do this is you force auto launch. In this case, there's no reminder and the the reflection agent will just get launched automatically. So yeah, I I think this is kind of subjective which one you want to choose. # Meetups & Talks

# Letta Discord Hackathon: Personality Design

**Video ID:** ZBQqiV01GSQ
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZBQqiV01GSQ

---

## Transcript

Hello, my name is Cameron Fiper and I work here at Leta. Brief overview on the Discord Hackathon. As you can see, the words are written up here. You will have 48 hours starting on Friday, September 5th at 6:00 p.m. Pacific Central time. The general theme of this contest is personality design. So, stateful agents are different than most other forms of agents or language models or whatever it is you work with. Stateful agents develop. They grow. They learn. They evolve. You design a memory architecture. You talk to them. You correct them on their behavior. You give them different kinds of tools. You put them in different environments. And over time, your agent will develop a personality. It will become more stable. it will start developing personality quirks, things that you might enjoy about it or dislike about it. But your stateful agents become more and more of a distinct entity the longer you spend working. So the goal of personality design is to focus on this aspect of creating a stateful agent that's actually interesting or good or useful. And so we're going to measure this in this hackathon using three categories. If you know anything about language models or working with AI in general, you will know that language models are not funny. It's very hard to get them to be funny. So, our first category here is going to be to try to create an agent that is actually capable of making people laugh in some meaningful way. You can choose to do this any way you want, but any agent that you submit to this category should actually be funny in some kind of way. And that doesn't have to be how it can talk, but its actions or how it thinks or anything like that should be humorous in some way. Useful. And I apologize for my handwriting. I'm not good at handwriting. Useful agents are agents that accomplish a task in a useful way. Hopefully, they're not annoying, but your agent should have some kind of capability, something that it can do in the real world that is actually meaningful, that solves a problem. Agents that you submit to this category should be functional. They should accomplish a goal. Decide what that goal is and then design your agent to accomplish that goal in a clear way. Number three, stful agents uh can be a form of art. You are developing a character. You're developing a personality. It may have a story arc as you work with it to develop its personality. It can be a novel concept implemented in an interesting way. This is a category where you can kind of explore artistic expression for artificial intelligence. Ideally, your agent that you submit to this category should be interesting. Somebody should look at your agent and sit up and think, I haven't seen that before. I don't know if it's particularly useful. I don't think it's particularly funny, but there's something about this agent that feels different. And whether that can be because you know if you know anything that I work on, I work on void which is a social agent on the blue sky network that remembers everybody. Um people would describe void as being distinct. It feels weird. It can be funny sometimes. It's not particularly useful, but it is creative. And if you want to build an agent that is a creative and functional entity, right, submit it to the third category. You may submit one agent per category. One agent per category. You don't have to submit to all three. You can spend most of your time building just one agent very well. And I do recommend that, you know, for people because if you haven't done a lot of stateful agent design, it may be very difficult to be competitive in all three of these categories. At the end of the hackathon, you will submit your agents along with a 3minute video and or a brief write up of what your agent is and what it does. You should also submit the agent file that goes along with your agent. Agent files and letter are just a way of like snapshotting an agent so that you can give it to somebody else so they can download it and run with it. If you are interested, you can also make your agents public. We have an upcoming uh thing called the agent file directory that is a public way of essentially trading stateful agents. If you would like to make your agent public, you can let us know and we will give you the functionality to publish it. That would also be fantastic. Within each of these categories, you will be judged basically on um how well you fit the category. Like does your agent actually make me laugh? You will also be judged on actually using LEA very well. So if you kind of explore memory architecture, if you have a creative use of tools, anything like this will get you kind of bumped up. Your agent should generally be of a high quality. It should feel like somebody invested time into it. I will also be participating. So there's a shark in the tank. I can also help you set up a social agent. If you want to run something on Blue Sky, just let me know. I'll steer some code around and kind of get you through getting your agent up and running. And now comes the most important part, which is prizes. First, there will be a $250 reward or $250 in equivalent value. If you're outside of the US, it may be difficult for us to send you dollars. So, we'll probably try and send you something that sort of works like $250. That is per category. If you win all three categories, you will get $750. Not bad. We will send you swag. So, right now, a lot of the swag that we have is like cups and stuff. Uh, so you'll get some nice cups and mugs and things like that. We will have shirts later. We will send like shirts to uh to winners when we have like actual shirts. They're very nice. Um, so you will enjoy them. So, you will get swag. You will get credibility. Everyone will think you're cool. And, uh, more seriously, people don't really know how to build stateful agents. They're so new. Um, most people are not familiar with the fact that you can build a distinct artificial intelligence entity with memory with separate skills with a history and understanding how to make something funny or useful or creative actually gets you really far in the world of AI. So, we'll blast you around on all the social media channels and we might do like an interview or something like this, but this is a good way to showcase the fact that you understand something that's completely new. These are self-improving artificial intelligence entities and you can be at the frontier of exploring how they work. And so, this is a great opportunity to showcase your ability to understand something. Recap of timelines. You will have Friday at 6:00 p.m. Pacific time. Versus with a staple agent you instead have this notion of state. So this is maybe persisted locally, maybe it's persisted in some database. And then from this state, you essentially compile a context window. So maybe this is through an LLM, some algorithm you have. You essentially compile a context window to represent the most important parts of your state. Maybe it's recent messages, maybe it's memories that your agent has stored. You essentially compile that context window to most accurately reflect that state. Send it to the LM. The LM maybe generates new messages, maybe it takes new actions, and that of course modifies the state. So this kind of looks a lot more like that sort of classical like RL definition of an agent. And so why why do we care? Why do we actually want SQL agents? Well, um first of all, it enables agents that can actually learn from experience through their state. This was an example that we had in the, you know, original MEGBT paper where it's like, you know, if you have this chatbot that's asking how's James doing and then the user is like, actually, James and I broke up, then you probably want to replace that information boyfriend named James to ex-boyfriend named James so that you don't keep on asking the same question about James over and over again. Um, another example is, you know, if you want to have agents that actually can like learn from your data. So maybe you have a bunch of like internal like company data or enterprise data. # Model Reviews

# An overview of Claude Haiku 4.5

**Video ID:** Xiw4cFc8Tx0
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Xiw4cFc8Tx0

---

## Transcript

Hey everyone, Cameron here. Atleta Anthropic today released Claude Haiku 4.5, which is their newest, smallest model. It's been almost a year since we got our last Haiku release, and it's a very good model according to their writeup and benchmarks. It's a third of the cost of Sonnet, which is not saying much. Sonnet is very expensive. It's $1 per million input tokens and $5 per million output tokens. And it's currently the cheapest model you can get from Anthropic. It matches Claude Sonnet 4's performance from about 5 months ago, which was a very good model at the time. Haiku also has an extended thinking mode for harder problems, which can be really valuable in coding tasks. It's also good at computer use. So if you use something like claude for Chrome, you may start seeing some additional benefits in using Haiku. Haiku should be faster for most of your use cases, but I wasn't really able to find kind of like good numbers about how much faster. I've seen estimates between two times faster than Sonnet and four to five times faster than Sonnet. # Exploring Claude Sonnet 4.5's powerful new memory tool

**Video ID:** pidnIHdA1Y8
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pidnIHdA1Y8

---

## Transcript

Hi everyone, this is Charles from Leta. Today I'm going to be talking about Anthropic's brand new model release, Sonnet 4.5. And specifically, I'm going to be focusing on this new tool they added and they a tool that they train the model to be very good at called the memory tool. I think the memory tool is probably the most interesting um aspect of this model release. I mean, this is, you know, a new flagship model release. So there's of course you know various scores that you know Sonet 4.5 is presumably better better than um you know even Opus but clearly you know Sonet 4 and its direct predecessor you know better at various tasks but I think the most interesting aspect of the this release is actually not just these benchmark scores but this training around the memory tool. So specifically there is now this new memory tool. Um, it's kind of like an official anthropic tool that Sonnet 4.5 has been trained to use very well. And you can see that this memory tool is actually basically treating the agents long-term memory as a file directory. And I think this kind of makes a lot of sense given uh the trend of how models have been trained to be better and better at coding over time. Um but functionally what this means is that you you can now kind of enable this tool with sonnet 4.5 and basically the model will kind of try to use this memories directory as its long-term memory bank. This is kind of like an extension of the agents MD idea or the claw.mmd file. So yeah, if you're familiar with the coding agents, if you use, you know, claw code, cursor, codeex, um, for a while now, maybe like six, 12 months, there's been this concept of like the the markdown file that you're going to put in your directory. Um, a popular one now is called like agents.mmd. I think this is supported by yeah, kind of supported by the majority of coding agents apart from cloud code. Um, but yeah, you basically make this file in your repo and then you describe you kind of add things. These are kind of like memories or but more like instructions for an agent for how to use your code or how to use your repo. Um so yeah, in this case you were kind of describing to the agent how to set up the entire repo.
